method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island lies about 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean at approximately 0°22′S 160°01′W, within the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands.[2] It is a small, uninhabited coral island formed by the crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano.[2] The island’s interior is a dried former lagoon where deep guano deposits accumulated, reflecting its long history as a seabird nesting site.[2]

The land is low, mostly sandy, and reaches only about 7 m (23 ft) above sea level at its highest point.[2] Jarvis has a tropical desert climate marked by high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sunlight, scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 in) per year, and no known natural freshwater lens.[2] Its terrestrial vegetation is sparse and consists mainly of bunch grasses, vines, and low-growing shrubs, with no plants larger than shrubs.[2] These low plant communities provide nesting, roosting, and sheltering habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and numerous land hermit crabs.[2]

The emergent land area of Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge is about 1,273 acres, while submerged lands and waters within the three-mile boundary add about 36,214 acres, for a total refuge area of 37,487 acres.[3] The refuge was established with the stated purpose of preserving the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, with special emphasis on large seabird nesting colonies.[3] Jarvis is described by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as one of the few tropical island ecosystems where terrestrial and marine ecological processes remain relatively intact and largely free of human impact.[3]

Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] The island has been identified as having 14 species of breeding seabirds and as the only protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[3] Jarvis alone has been estimated to support about one million sooty terns, making its small area of emergent land especially important for seabird reproduction in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument region.[1] Following eradication of invasive mammals, bird populations on Jarvis and nearby islands increased, with shearwaters recorded as returning to Jarvis.[1]

The surrounding marine ecosystem is unusually productive because of wind-driven equatorial upwelling combined with topographic upwelling of eastward-flowing currents around the island.[2] The shallow benthic habitats around Jarvis include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the island’s eastern windward side.[2] The fringing reef has been characterized as a healthy coral community, a condition attributed in part to the island’s remoteness and limited anthropogenic disturbance.[3] Jarvis is also considered an important site for studying long-term global climate change and periodic oceanographic phenomena such as the El Niño–Southern Oscillation.[3]

==History==
Jarvis Island was first sighted by Europeans on August 21, 1821, when Captain Brown of the British ship *Eliza Francis* encountered it; the ship was owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, for whom the island was named.[2][4] It was also historically known as Bunker Island or Bunker's Shoal.[4]

American interest in Jarvis grew after guano deposits were identified by whalers and other seafarers in the central Pacific, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed the island in 1840.[2] In 1857, Arthur Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of Jarvis for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Later that year, Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* surveyed the island and formally claimed it for the United States.[4] Guano was mined on Jarvis from 1857 until 1879, when the island was abandoned after substantial quantities had been removed.[4][5]

The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not develop mining operations there.[4][5] In 1906, the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, although mining again was not further developed.[4] The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in 1935.[4][5] On May 13, 1936, President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 7368, placing control and jurisdiction of the island under the Secretary of the Interior.[3]

During World War II, Japanese forces attacked the island, forcing the evacuation of its colonists.[1] On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its territorial sea out to three nautical miles as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also included within the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service administers Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System, applying the system-wide mission of conserving, managing and, where appropriate, restoring fish, wildlife, plants and their habitats for present and future generations.[2][5] USFWS describes the refuge as encompassing 429,853 acres, including 1,273 acres of terrestrial area and 428,580 acres of submerged lands.[2] The World Database on Protected Areas reports a closely corresponding protected-area extent of 1,744.87 km² and classifies the site as a marine and coastal protected area.[1] Earlier refuge planning documents described the legal boundary as the island plus its territorial sea to the three-mile limit, totaling 37,487 acres, before later descriptions used the larger acreage.[5][2]

Management planning for the refuge has been conducted through USFWS comprehensive conservation planning and environmental assessment documents, including a 2008 comprehensive conservation plan listed for Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge.[4][5] The draft plan compared management alternatives and summarized proposed actions, environmental effects and estimated annual costs for field-camp configurations.[5] Access and proposed uses are treated as compatibility matters; for example, USFWS issued a draft compatibility determination for amateur-radio operations and concluded that the activity would not detract from protection of the refuge's natural character and associated biological resources.[2] Agency field work is intermittent because of the island's remoteness; a USFWS Pacific monument biology team visit in August 2024 was described as the first Service trip to Jarvis in more than six years.[3]

==Transportation==
Access to Jarvis Island is limited to marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean travel, which are described as the only means of transportation to the island.[3] The island lies about 1,263 nautical miles from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service management staff in Honolulu, and the voyage to Jarvis takes about eight days by ship on average.[3] Transportation has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels.[3] NOAA scientific expeditions typically visit Jarvis as part of multi-island research cruises to central Pacific insular refuges, often in combination with Baker, Howland, and Johnston islands.[3] Operational access is constrained by the island’s remoteness, lack of affordable and reliable transportation, lack of infrastructure for field operations, extreme environmental conditions, and safety and landing limitations.[3] The island’s low-lying terrain, shallow fringing reef, steep offshore slopes, sandy western beach, and wave-exposed eastern shore further shape landing and field logistics.[1] Public use and access to the refuge are closed except for authorized management, research, or permitted activities.[3]

==Military==
Military considerations were one reason for the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, which placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis and other central Pacific islands so that weather stations and potential landing fields could support military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[1] The project also reflected U.S. concern over Japanese territorial expansion in the eastern Pacific before World War II.[1] Physical traces associated with historical military activity include short channels blasted through Jarvis’s shallow fringing reefs to improve small-boat access.[4] U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service planning documents also note that hazards may remain from human habitation during the island’s guano-mining and military eras.[3]
